title: From Prior to Pro: Efficient Skill Mastery via Distribution Contractive RL Finetuning
abstract: We introduce Distribution Contractive Reinforcement Learning (DICE-RL), a framework that uses reinforcement learning (RL) as a "distribution contraction" operator to refine pretrained generative robot policies. DICE-RL turns a pretrained behavior prior into a high-performing "pro" policy by amplifying high-success behaviors from online feedback. We pretrain a diffusion- or flow-based policy for broad behavioral coverage, then finetune it with a stable, sample-efficient residual off-policy RL framework that combines selective behavior regularization with value-guided action selection. Extensive experiments and analyses show that DICE-RL reliably improves performance with strong stability and sample efficiency. It enables mastery of complex long-horizon manipulation skills directly from high-dimensional pixel inputs, both in simulation and on a real robot. Project website: [dice.rl.2026](https://zhanyisun.github.io/dice.rl.2026/).
decision_comment:
### Summary
This paper presents a practical recipe for fine-tuning a behavioral cloning (BC) robotics policy using reinforcement learning (RL). Empirical evaluation is performed on some robomimic benchmarks as well as a real robot task (belt assembly).

### Meta-Review
The proposed recipe for fine-tuning the robot control policy after pre-training via BC seems to be empirically effective, and evaluation on a real robot task lends credence to the proposed approach. Ablations and experimental probes lay bare how the design choices affect the performance of the proposed approach. There should be more discussion on the comparison of the proposed technique with ResFit, since not seeing the difference in the two techniques was a point that the reviewers brought up.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes DICE-RL, a distribution contractive reinforcement learning (RL) finetuning framework for pretrained generative robot behavior cloning (BC) policies. It freezes the pretrained BC backbone, learns a lightweight action-chunk-level residual policy, and integrates a selective BC-loss filter, multi-sample training and value-guided action selection for stable policy refinement. Experiments on Robomimic benchmarks and real robot assembly tasks show DICE-RL outperforms mainstream baselines with higher success rate, stability and sample efficiency.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. The framework strategically integrates the strengths of prior residual correction, distillation-style regularization and steering-style value-guided selection, with a well-motivated "distribution contraction" design highly practical for real-world robotic deployment.
2. The evaluation is comprehensive, covering simulation and real robot tasks across state and pixel inputs, with systematic ablations validating core module effectiveness and ensuring result reproducibility.
3. The work reveals the sharpening-contraction effect of RL finetuning on pretrained action distributions, and provides actionable guidance for BC policy pretraining and downstream finetuning.

Weakness:
1. The rationale for the core design choice of per-chunk residual is not sufficiently justified. The highly relevant baseline ResFit adopts a per-step residual design and qualitatively discusses the advantages of per-step over per-chunk residual in its work, while this paper does not provide an explicit explanation for choosing the per-chunk scheme.
2. The innovation boundary between the proposed method and the most relevant prior work ResFit needs further clarification. The current ablation study does not fully disentangle the performance impact of the core BC-loss filter from other design differences, and it is unclear how the method would perform against ResFit when the core module is removed.
3. The method is only validated on single-task BC pretraining and finetuning settings, and its applicability to multi-task VLA model finetuning (as explored in CO-RFT, Huang et al., 2025) has not been discussed, leaving the generalizability and scalability of the framework to be further explored.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Questions:
1. Could the authors explain the rationale for adopting the per-chunk residual design instead of the widely used per-step residual scheme in related works like ResFit?
2. The paper notes that ResFit does not impose an explicit BC-style regularization during finetuning as the key difference from DICE-RL. Could the authors explain the core reasons for the performance gap between DICE-RL and ResFit under the setting of β=0 (i.e., without BC filter loss)?
3. Could the authors discuss the potential adaptability of the DICE-RL framework to finetuning large multi-task VLA models, and what key modifications may be needed for this application
reviews.limitations: The authors should include a discussion of limitations in the paper.
reviews.final_justification: Although the author has addressed some of the issues I raised, others cannot be resolved in the short term, so I have decided to keep the current “weak reject” score unchanged.

reviews.summary: DICE-RL treats RL as a post-training mechanism for a pretrained generative behavior policy rather than learning a robot policy from scratch. The method freezes the pretrained policy, learns a residual off-policy actor with selective BC-style regularization, and uses value-guided best-of-K action selection during interaction. The paper supports the approach with Robomimic experiments, a real-world belt assembly task, and appendix ablations on pretraining quality, convergence status, sampling, and implementation details.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

- The paper is built around a well-defined problem setting, and its central claim is clearly articulated: in robotic scenarios with sparse rewards, long horizons, and limited online interaction budgets, RL is better suited as a post-training stage for pretrained generative behavior-cloning policies rather than as a mechanism for exploration from scratch.

- The method design is technically sound and shows strong engineering practicality. By freezing the pretrained generative policy, learning only a residual actor, and combining this with a TD3+BC-style objective, a filtered BC regularizer, and best-of-K value-guided selection, the method strikes a reasonable balance between preserving prior support and allowing necessary deviations.

- The experiments cover both state-based and pixel-based observations, multiple long-horizon Robomimic tasks, and one real-robot BeltAssembly task. In addition to final success rates, the main paper also reports training stability and sample efficiency, which makes the empirical evaluation more informative.

- The authors go beyond simply showing that the method works. They also systematically analyze which kinds of pretrained policies are more amenable to subsequent RL finetuning, including factors such as demonstration quality, checkpoint training stage, number of demonstrations, and metrics such as good mode coverage and bad mode entropy. This part provides additional insight into the method and adds interpretive value.

Weaknesses

- Most of the major components—residual policy correction, off-policy actor-critic learning, behavior regularization, value-guided candidate selection, and offline-online mixing—can be traced to existing lines of work. As a result, the paper reads more as a strong integrated solution for generative BC post-training than as a fundamentally new methodological contribution.

- The theoretical support is relatively limited. Although the paper emphasizes “distribution sharpening” and “contraction,” these notions function more as empirical characterizations and post hoc interpretations than as rigorously established theoretical guarantees. The explanations for stability and sample efficiency still rely primarily on experimental observations.

- If the phrase “skill mastering” in the title is intended as a strong claim, the current evidence remains somewhat limited. The main paper evaluates on four Robomimic tasks plus one real-world belt assembly task, and the task diversity, robot platforms, and reward settings are still fairly concentrated.

- The real-robot experiments are persuasive, but they rely on human supervision, manual resets, and binary success labels. The paper would benefit from a more explicit quantification of real-world wall-clock cost, the level of human involvement, and sensitivity to labeling consistency.

- The paper could also position itself more clearly with respect to recent work on generative policy post-training, including the limitations of fair comparison, as well as its relationship to broader robotics post-training and RLVR-style framings.

-The title of Section 5.5  “Ablations Studies” -> “Ablation Studies.”
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- The filtered BC regularizer relies on a joint gating mechanism based on critic predictions and Monte Carlo return estimates. Why this heuristic is sufficient to reliably prevent exploitation of critic overestimation is still explained mainly at an empirical level rather than in a principled way.

- In the real-robot setting, the effects of human intervention frequency, early termination policies for failed trials, and the consistency of reward labeling are not analyzed in sufficient detail.

- The relationship between the proposed finetunability metrics—good coverage, bad coverage, and bad entropy—and final RL performance is intuitive, but the paper does not yet explain their threshold choices, cross-task robustness, or broader generalizability clearly enough.
reviews.final_justification:
My main concerns have been addressed with useful clarifications and additional empirical evidence.

1. **Method positioning and novelty.** The rebuttal clarifies that the contribution is not centered on the novelty of each individual component, but on a coherent post-training design guided by distribution contraction/sharpening. This framing makes the paper’s contribution clearer and more internally consistent.

2. **Theoretical claims.** The authors appropriately clarified that contraction is intended as a design rationale rather than a theorem-level guarantee. This substantially improves the rigor of the presentation and resolves my concern that the original wording was somewhat stronger than what is formally supported.

3. **Empirical support.** The added evidence, including the new real-world clarifications and the additional results beyond the original single-task setting, strengthens the empirical support of the paper and improves confidence in the practical usefulness of the method.

4. **Scope and presentation.** The rebuttal also addressed my concerns about overly strong wording and metric interpretation, including the title revision and the clarification of the scope of the finetunability-related metrics.

That said, my original reservations about the overall level of novelty and the lack of stronger theoretical guarantees are only partially alleviated, as these aspects are mainly clarified rather than fundamentally changed.

Overall, the rebuttal significantly improves the clarity, positioning, and empirical support of the work. Based on these improvements, I am inclined to slightly increase my assessment.

reviews.summary: This paper presents an approach for fine-tuning diffusion policies via “distribution contracting.” In particular, the paper freezes the base diffusion policy and instead learns a residual policy to correct actions from the base policy. The paper introduces a filtering mechanism to determine when to apply BC regularization on the residual policy’s updates. Empirically, the paper evaluates on both simulation, specifically Robomimic manipulation tasks, and real-robot experiments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Overall, my concerns are primarily with respect to soundness (specifically the implementation of the baselines) and originality (specifically the novelty over ResFit). 
1. Regarding soundness, on line 264: “[when comparing to DSRL] DICE-RL can both optimize the latent noise and apply a learnable residual action correction…”. How does DICE-RL optimize the latent noise? From my understanding, DICE-RL does not modify the latent noised passed into the base policy; it only passes the latent noise into the residual policy. 
2. Regarding the ResFit baseline, does the paper experiment with the RLPD-style offline/online mixing that you use for DICE-RL and DSRL? 
3. The paper did not include results for DSRL without the offline/online mixing schedule. The original DSRL paper did not include the mixing schedule, so this deviation from the original implementation deserves justification (and specifically, a comparison of both variations). 
4. The idea to measure metrics about the pretrained policy and data and observe their correlation with downstream performance is interesting. However, the actual metrics computed have issues. Regarding the measure of good mode coverage, this quantity is dependent on the learned value function, which undermines how valuable it is as a proxy metric for measuring the pretrained policy’s finetunability. Furthermore, if BC optimization is performed perfectly, then we would expect this measure to converge to 1. A more informative metric would be to estimate the pretrained policy’s Q function via MC rollouts in the environment, thus bypassing the critic dependence. While this requires environment interactions, these metrics are used for analysis, not for the actual algorithm. 
5. The BoK action sampling procedure effectively results in another soft policy iteration - biasing more towards actions that optimize Q (and potentially further away from the behavior of the behavior/pretrained policy). A more informative ablation on BoK action sampling (Figure 11) would be to plot K on the X axis and success rate on the Y axis. Each line could be performance at a different number of environment steps. Thus, we can observe if there is a trend with K (e.g. using too large K reduces performance due to selecting over-optimistic actions). 
6. As the performance of the pretrained policy improves, we’d expect to need less improvement over the base policy and thus fewer/smaller corrections. Methods like DSRL that simply modify the latent noise passed into the diffusion policy would likely become more effective. On the ablation over amount of pretraining data, including a baseline like DSRL would be informative. 
7. The purpose of FILTER is to allow the RL component to improve over the base policy on actions that are genuinely than the base sample. I have two major concerns: the second component of FILTER seems likely to have high variance, since $\hat{G}$ is a Monte-Carlo estimate. Furthermore, FILTER seems redundant when using both $L_{RL}$ and $L_{BC}$. Early in training, it seems like FILTER will only “work” when a^pre is bad, since a^cur cannot outperform the MC return estimate in the replay buffer (if the critic is learned well). If the pretrained policy is trained well, we would expect $Q(s, a^{pre}) \approx \hat{G}(s)$. So the implementation of FILTER seems less about allowing for meaningful improvements over the base policy and more about allowing the residual policy to correct the pretrained policy when the pretrained policy does not appropriately learn the training data. 
8. Pretraining is limited to single-task settings. In practice, the pretraining → post-training paradigm is typically used in multi-task settings. Extending the experiments to consider multi-task pretraining and single-task post-training seems like an important direction to consider. 
9. On the originality side, from my understanding, the only difference between ResFit and DICE-RL algorithms seems to be the BC regularization.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Is the $K$ used for BoK action sampling during inference the same as the “K” used for multi-sample training? What is it in the ablation Figure 11?
2. Can you explain in more detail the difference between ResFit and DICE-RL? 
3. Regarding the ResFit baseline, does the paper experiment with the RLPD-style offline/online mixing that you use for DICE-RL and DSRL?
reviews.final_justification: I maintained my score principally because of the lack of novelty and differentiation over the ResFit baseline. As the authors note in their rebuttal, the primary distinction from ResFit is adding chunking to the residual policy and adding a BC regularization loss. While the authors added ablations showing these additions improve over ResFit, both of these tools (action chunking and BC regularization losses) are standard, common practices, and I largely think of them as improvements to ResFit, rather than a standalone ICML paper. I appreciate the authors' rebuttal and they largely addressed my other concerns.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Distribution Contractive Reinforcement Learning (DICE-RL), an offline-to-online RL framework built upon a pretrained diffusion/flow model policy. It fine-tunes the residual policy in a sample-efficient manner using proper regularization and value guidance. The training pipeline is meticulously designed, incorporating techniques such as Adaptive RLPD mixing and a BC loss filter. The performance of this method is consistently robust across both simulation and real-world robot tasks. Furthermore, the paper presents several interesting findings, such as the "Contraction" phenomenon: the BC policy sharpens during the RL process, suggesting that it is advantageous to use a pretrained policy that has not been fully trained to convergence.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Soundness:
The paper demonstrates a robust offline-to-online RL framework, DICE-RL, for fine-tuning pretrained diffusion and flow-based policies. The proposed training pipeline, which incorporates techniques like Adaptive RLPD mixing and BC loss filtering, appears technically sound and meticulously engineered. The empirical results across both simulation and challenging real-world robotic tasks convincingly validate the sample efficiency and stability of the method. Furthermore, the analysis regarding the "contraction" phenomenon is insightful. I encourage the authors to provide more detailed ablation studies towards these phenomenon.

Presentation:
The paper is well-organized and clearly written. 

Significance:
This paper significantly advances our practical and theoretical understanding of offline-to-online RL fine-tuning for generative behavior cloning policies. By achieving high sample efficiency and stability, it addresses a major bottleneck in robot learning. I encourage the authors to delve deeper into the broader landscape of foundation models and explore the extension of this framework to large multi-task Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies, as briefly mentioned in their conclusion.

Originality:
This paper provides practical RL fine-tuning method for generative policies. The carefully designed pipeline distinguishes this work from existing literature in the offline-to-online RL domain.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: - In Fig.2, I noticed many baselines method fails in some tasks. Can you explain the failure mode in detail, tell which different design that makes DICE-RL work.
reviews.limitations: The authors have not discussed the limitations.
reviews.final_justification: My main concerns have been addressed so I raised my score.
